coredata.dc:title: Synchronously integration of carbon-quantum-dot and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped in Ni3S2@Ni foam toward robust and stable electrocatalytic hydrogen generation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaomei
Yang, Bilu
Xu, Zhaodi
Mo, Qiaoling
Shen, Xiaomei
Cai, Hu
coredata.dc:description: In the pursuit of advancing the production of H2 through hydrogen-evolution reaction (HER), the demand for the elaborate design of cost-effective and robust durability electrocatalysts has gained momentum, especially replacement of noble-metal counterparts like Pt. Herein, we introduce a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach to synthesize nickel foam-supported Ni3S2 nanosheet arrays, which integrate with carbon-quantum-dot (CQDs) and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped. With the introduction of CQDs and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped site, the optimal CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits superior HER catalytic activity with a low overpotential of 125 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 and strong durability for 30 h in alkaline medium. The exceptional electrocatalytic performance is ascribed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Cu, Mo dual-metal site. This synergy encompasses optimization of water molecule adsorption/desorption energetics, elevated electrical conductivity, weakening of the strong S–H bond interaction on the Ni3S2 surface, and providing ample active centers. Furthermore, an in-depth understanding of the electronic structure and adsorption energy during the HER process is probed by density functional theory (DFT) analysis. Our work provides a quintessential paradigm for the rational design of multi-component electrocatalysts for water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-precious dual-metal doped
Carbon quantum dots
Ni3S2@NF
Hydrogen evolution reaction

This work is funded by the National Natural Science Fund of China (21861026, 22075123). And this work was also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22007041, 22367013), and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20211BAB213019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF; (b & c) SEM images of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF; (d & e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF; (g) HAADF-STEM and corresponding elemental mapping of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF; (h) EDX pattern taken from such CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF nanostructures.
SEM images of (a–c) CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, (d–f) CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, and (g–i) CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF.
(a) Survey XPS spectra and high resolution XPS spectra of (b) Ni 2p, (c) S 2p, (d) Cu 2p, (e) Mo 3d and (f) C1s for CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF.

(a) LSV profiles of the as-prepared electrodes in 1 M KOH solution without iR compensation. (b) Comparison of the HER performance of NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF and Pt electrodes at −10, −50, −100 and −200 mA cm−2. (c) Corresponding Tafel slopes of NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF and Pt. (d) Calculated Cdl values and (e) Nyquist plots of NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF. (f) LSV curves of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF before and after 30 h durability test (inset: chronoamperometric curve of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF at the overpotential of 131 mV and 201 mV).

DFT calculation results for the HER catalytic mechanisms of bare and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF. (a) Geometry structure of the Mo–Cu–Ni3S2. (b) Projected density of state (PDOS) and the crystal orbital Hamilton populations (COHP) analysis for Mo–Cu–Ni3S2. (c) The free energy curve for HER simulation process on the surface of Ni3S2-based catalysts. (d) Adsorption energy for H2O on different sites of the Ni3S2-based catalysts. (e) The differential charge density of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, the yellow-colored isosurfaces denote electron accumulation, and the blue-colored isosurfaces denote electron depletion. (f) Density of states of Cu–Mo–Ni3S2@NF and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF.

head.ce:title: Synchronously integration of carbon-quantum-dot and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped in Ni3S2@Ni foam toward robust and stable electrocatalytic hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, 330029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the pursuit of advancing the production of H2 through hydrogen-evolution reaction (HER), the demand for the elaborate design of cost-effective and robust durability electrocatalysts has gained momentum, especially replacement of noble-metal counterparts like Pt. Herein, we introduce a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach to synthesize nickel foam-supported Ni3S2 nanosheet arrays, which integrate with carbon-quantum-dot (CQDs) and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped. With the introduction of CQDs and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped site, the optimal CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits superior HER catalytic activity with a low overpotential of 125 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 and strong durability for 30 h in alkaline medium. The exceptional electrocatalytic performance is ascribed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Cu, Mo dual-metal site. This synergy encompasses optimization of water molecule adsorption/desorption energetics, elevated electrical conductivity, weakening of the strong S–H bond interaction on the Ni3S2 surface, and providing ample active centers. Furthermore, an in-depth understanding of the electronic structure and adsorption energy during the HER process is probed by density functional theory (DFT) analysis. Our work provides a quintessential paradigm for the rational design of multi-component electrocatalysts for water splitting.

Homogeneous carbon-quantum-dot-modified Cu and Mo dual metal doped Ni3S2@NF electrodes are constructed by a simple yet effective one-pot hydrothermal strategy for hydrogen-evolution reaction.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the escalating global energy crisis and growing environmental concerns have underscored the imperative to develop eco-friendly and renewable energy [1–3]. Hydrogen as an ideal energy fuel has attracted ever-growing interest. The current primary approach to hydrogen generation involves coal gasification, steam reforming, and electrochemical water splitting [4]. Especially electrochemical water splitting is considered as a clean technique with zero carbon emission and high energy density to produce sustainable hydrogen production [5–7]. Platinum-based materials are generally considered among the cutting-edge electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to their thermally neutral hydrogen adsorption free energy almost on the metal surfaces [8–10]. Nonetheless, their limited availability and high cost have restricted their practical application. Therefore, the rational design of non-Pt metal-based materials as efficient HER electrocatalysts has emerged as a challenging research pursuit.

Among potential non-precious metal catalysts, Ni3S2-based materials as effective HER catalysts have been prepared in a series of recent studies [11–13]. Ni3S2, a representative of transition metal catalyst, possesses intrinsic metallic features due to the existence of the continuous network of Ni–Ni bonds throughout its structure, which facilitates rapid charge transfer to some extent [14]. Feng et al. [15] demonstrated the potential of high-index faceted Ni3S2 nanosheet arrays for water splitting, highlighting their impressive durability (>200 h) and cost-effectiveness (∼100% Faradaic yield). This makes them a promising alternative to noble-metal-based catalysts in electrocatalysis. Ouyang et al. [16] reported hierarchically porous Ni3S2 nanorod arrays on Ni foam, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity and stability in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) with a low overpotential of 200 mV at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2. It's worth noting, however, that Ni3S2 has a weak electrochemical adsorption capacity for water molecules at the Ni sites, which hinders water molecule dissociation. Simultaneously, the formation of S-Hads bonds on the catalyst surfaces promote H adsorption. Still, the transfer of Hads to H2 is challenging in alkaline media (Volmer step, M + H2O + e− = M−Had + OH−). Furthermore, limited active sites, weak stability and restricted conductivity have impeded their electrocatalytic property.

Fortunately, considerable research efforts have been dedicated to exploring various strategies for enhancing electrocatalytic performance, including doping engineering [17,18], morphology control [19,20], and heterostructure construction [21,22]. Given the inherent catalytic prowess of nickel-based catalysts, element doping has emerged as a well-established and effective strategy. This approach aims to alter the electronic state, rearrange valence electrons, and fine-tune the free energy associated with hydrogen adsorption. For now, several metals, including Co [23], Cu [24], Mo [25], and Fe [26], have been reported as efficient dopants to augment electrocatalytic properties. Doping with transition metals can improve the adsorption and activation of H2O while optimizing the adsorption and desorption of H, as compared to pure Ni3S2 for HER. The doped metals can be regarded as the electron donor, thereby regulating the electronic structure of the catalyst, promoting the density of states, and reducing the energy barrier for water dissociation, ultimately strengthening the HER activity. Nevertheless, the majority of these studies have focused on single-metal doping.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), characterized by their zero-dimensional (0D) structure, have garnered significant attention within the family of carbon-based materials [27]. This heightened interest can be attributed to its numerous advantages, such as cost-effectiveness, a larger surface area, abundant surface function groups, and higher electrical conductivity [28,29]. The heteroatom in the CQDs allows for the regulate of charge distribution, thereby improving conductivity and electrocatalytic performance through internal interactions [30]. More importantly, the abundance of functional groups (such as –OH, –COOH) on CQDs surfaces enable coordination with metal ions possessing empty d orbitals, resulting in the formation of stable CQDs-metal ion coordination materials [31]. Zhang et al. [30] reported the synthesis of nanohybrids (CQDs/MoP) by the multi-step self-assembly strategy and phosphatization, and it required 210 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of 20 mA·cm−2. The introduction of CQD alleviates the agglomeration and surface oxidation, significantly improving the intrinsic catalytic activity of MoP and exhibiting high efficiency synergistic catalysis. Significantly, most CQDs-based materials typically involve a multi-step preparation process, which can be relatively complex. Therefore, it is imperative to develop simpler and more effective synthetic methods.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF electrocatalyst by employing a one-pot hydrothermal method to integrate CQDs and Cu, Mo dual-metal doped. The Cu/Mo ratio, l-cysteine content, and hydrothermal conditions were systematically tuned to control the structure and morphology of Ni3S2@NF. The CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF electrocatalyst exhibits several merits: (1) The utilization of Ni foam as a substrate serves a dual purpose by furnishing a substantial reservoir of nickel atoms and facilitating the formation of self-supported Ni3S2 nanosheets, thereby eliminating the need for binders. (2) Cu and Mo dual-metal doped Ni3S2 nanosheets could modify the electronic structure and regulate the surface morphology. Through the optimization of Cu and Mo dual doping in varying proportions, the morphology of the material is tailored to enhance the exposure of active sites and increase the electrochemical active area. Additionally, the electronic structure is optimized by modifying the electron cloud density around Ni3S2. (3) Introducing CQDs to Cu–Mo–Ni3S2@NF electrocatalyst could promote electrochemical stability, enhance electrical conductivity, and optimize electronic structure further. By utilizing CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF as the HER electrode, the integrated electrolysis device achieves an impressively low overpotential of 131 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 for HER in an alkaline solution. Consequently, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibit exceptional HER electrocatalytic activity and remarkable long-term electrochemical stability, outperforming single/bi/tri-compound electrocatalysts. This one-step method provides an effective avenue for advancing HER electrocatalysts and offers insights for potential exploration across diverse catalytic applications.

Copper chloride (CuCl2) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sodium molybdate (Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O) was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent No.4 Factory. l-cysteine [HSCH2CH(NH2)CO2H] was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the chemical materials were directly used without any further puriﬁcation and the water was purified through the Millipore in the whole experiments.

In a typical synthesis, a solvothermal method was applied for fabricating the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF heterostructures, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF nanoparticles, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets, and CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets. The Ni foam substrate was sonicated in acetone and HCl (3 M) for 15 min to dismiss the surface NiO layer and then pretreated with deionized water and ethanol for 10 min. 0.3 mmol Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O, 5 mmol l-cysteine, and 0.45 mmol CuCl2 were dispersed in the mixture solution (25 mL water + 5 mL ethylene glycol) and vigorously stirred at room temperature to form a homogenous solution. Subsequently, the mixture solution was transferred in a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and the as-prepared nickel foam (2 × 3 cm) was inserted diagonally into the reactor, and then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling naturally, the obtained CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF heterostructures were rinsed with DI water and ethanol several times and dried on a vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

The loadings of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF were 20.0, 18.4, 16.6, and 13.8 mg/cm2, respectively. Electrocatalyst loadings reached saturation with the addition of 5 mmol l-cysteine (Fig. S1). Based on the molar ratio of l-cysteine to Cu–Mo, these were labeled as CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-2:1, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-1:1, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-1:3, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-1:5, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-1:7, and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF-1:9, corresponding to l-cysteine amounts of 0.06 g, 0.12 g, 0.36 g, 0.6 g, 0.84 g, and 1.08 g, respectively.

For comparison, the CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, and Ni3S2@NF were prepared. The CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF and CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF were synthesized in the same way of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, except that Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O and CuCl2 are not added, respectively. Similarly, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF was synthesized without Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O and CuCl2, while keeping all other experimental conditions consistent. In comparison to CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, Ni3S2@NF was synthesized under the same conditions, except that the l-cysteine precursor was replaced with sodium sulfide.

under the same conditions excluding without added CuCl2. Similarly, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets were synthesized without Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets were synthesized without Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O and CuCl2.

To explore the impact of diﬀerent molar ratio and temperatures on morphological structure and their electrochemical performance, specific Cu and Mo molar content (3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 3:2, and 1:3) were fabricated under the above conditions. Moreover, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets were prepared at different temperatures (120, 150, 180, and 200 °C).

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, FEI, QuanTA-200F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F) were utilized to analyze the internal structure and surface microstructures of samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB25OXi Instrument) was received for further analyzing the chemical states of all samples. The phase component was studied by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, Cu Ka = 1.5406 Å, 40 kV and 40 mA).

The electrochemical experiments were investigated in a three-electrode configuration, including the working electrode, reference electrode (standard Hg/HgO), and counter electrode (carbon rod). Analysis was performed to mitigate the influence of reduction peaks using the reverse scans of cyclic voltammograms (CV) from positive to negative potentials. The potential values of reference electrode were calculated using the equation ERHE = EHg/HgO + 0.098 + 0.059 × pH (1.0 M KOH, pH = 14). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were tested at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The long-term durability test was evaluated through chronoamperometric measurements. Nyquist plots were acquired using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements at an overpotential of 220 V over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 MHz. All the curves are no iR compensation in this study. The electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) value can be obtained from the linear slope of current density versus scan rate in the non-Faradaic region of the collected CV data. The CV measurements were conducted at different scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 mV/s) within the potential range of 0.1–0.2 V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). All the tests were performed under room temperature conditions.

Density functional theory (DFT) was employed to conduct structural optimization and static self-consistent calculations using the Vienna ab-into simulation package (VASP) code. Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) methods were used to describe the electron exchange correlation. The projector-augmented-wave (PAW) potentials were adopted to describe the electron-ion interactions. In all structural relaxations, long-range van der Waals interactions were corrected by the DFT-D3BJ method. The energy cutoff was set at 450 eV. The convergence tolerances were set at 10−5 eV for energy and 0.01 eV/Å for force. In this work, we constructed a 3 × 3 × 1 supercell of (110) surface with 6 atom layers, containing 40 atoms, as the Ni3S2 substrate. This specific surface has been experimentally observed to exhibit high activity for water splitting. To evaluate the HER performance, we assessed the Gibbs free energy of H adsorption (ΔGH*), which is defined by the following expression:
(1) ΔGH* = ΔEH* + ΔEZPE -TΔSH*
where ΔEH* corresponds to the hydrogen adsorption energy and obtained by
(2)
Δ E
H *
=
E
H *
−
E + 1 /
2 E
H 2
in which EH* and E represent the total energy of the slab model with and without the adsorbed H atom, and
E
H 2
denotes the energy of an H2 molecule in the gas phase. ΔEZPE and ΔSH denote the differences in zero-point energy and entropy between the adsorbed state and gaseous state of hydrogen, respectively. The temperature (T) is set at 298.15 K for our calculations.

The synthesis process of electrocatalysts is illustrated in Scheme 1 . We employed a simple one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs-loaded Cu, Mo dual-doped Ni3S2 nanosheets in situ on a Ni foam substrate. In this process, Ni foam and l-cysteine were utilized as the source of nickel and sulfur, while the incorporation of CQDs could further enhance the electrocatalytic properties. By precisely tuning the Cu and Mo ratios and optimizing the quantity of l-cysteine, we successfully engineered an interconnected network of nanosheets on the Ni foam substrate, resulting in the formation of the hybrid structure denoted as CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on the samples to identify the crystal structure. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the three strong peaks near 44.4°, 51.7°, and 76.3° are unambiguously assigned to the Ni foam (JCPDS No. 04–0850). For all samples, a series of well-defined diffraction peaks situated at 21.7°, 31.1°, 38°, 50.1°, and 55.2° are attributed to (101), (110), (003), (211), and (122) lattice planes of hexagonal Ni3S2 (JCPDS no. 44–1418) [32], indicating that the crystal structure of Ni3S2@NF remains intact subsequent to the introduction of CQDs and the incorporation of Cu/Mo dopants. Besides, as shown in the amplified XRD pattern (Fig. S2), the peaks corresponding to (110) in CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF display a noticeable shift to the lower angles when compared to Ni3S2@NF, suggesting Mo and Cu atoms are doped successfully. Notably, no discernible peaks associated with Cu or Mo sulfides are detected, suggesting that Cu and Mo atoms potentially adopt a stable doping configuration within the Ni3S2 lattice.

Based on the Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs and CQDs-Cu-Mo/Ni3S2@NF (Fig. S3), several prominent peaks can be identified at 3487 cm−1 (-OH), 2968 and 2921 cm−1 (C–H), 1649 cm−1 (CO), 1285 cm−1 (CC), 1076 and 833 cm−1 (C–O), confirming the successful formation of CQDs [33,34]. These results validate the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs that can interact with metal ions. In comparison to pure CQDs, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF composite exhibits a shift in the main peaks, with the CO peak shifting by approximately 29 cm−1. This shift demonstrates the existence of strong electrostatic interactions between Cu–Mo–Ni3S2@NF and CQDs, indicating physical absorption and the formation of new bonds (C–O-M, where M = Ni, Cu, or Mo) [35]. Additionally, the well-ordered Ni3S2 nanosheets, highly conductive GQDs, and the uniform distribution of Cu and Mo elements contribute to the formation of mismatches and interfaces, leading to the presence of abundant active edge sites and enhanced electrocatalytic activity.

The morphology and structure of electrocatalysts were thoroughly investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As disclosed in Fig. 1b&c, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits a distinctive structure characterized by a vertical array of ultra-thin nanosheets uniformly coating the surface. These nanosheets are intricately interconnected, creating an open and porous structure. This unique architecture offers several advantages: it facilitates the complete penetration and diffusion of the electrolyte, expedites the release of gases, increases the specific surface area, and provides a higher density of electrochemically active sites, ultimately leading to improved electrocatalytic activity. The TEM images of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF (Fig. 1d&e) also confirm the ultrathin nanosheets structure of Ni3S2@NF, onto which abundant CQDs are evenly attached on the Ni3S2 without agglomeration. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1f) reveals that the lattice fringes with interplanar distances of 0.254, 0.290, and 0.230 nm, matching with (120) crystal facts of CQDs and Ni3S2, respectively. The elemental mapping results of CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF (Fig. 1g&h) demonstrate the uniform distribution of Ni, S, Mo, Cu and C throughout the ultrathin nanostructure. This uniformity confirms the homogeneity of the Cu and Mo doping, as well as the decoration of CQDs on Ni3S2@NF. In addition, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis has identified trace amounts of Cu (0.3 at.%) and Mo (0.14 at.%) in the CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF.

The XRD and SEM results of CQDs-Cu-Mo/Ni3S2@NF at different hydrothermal temperatures (from 120 to 200 °C) and various molar ratios of Cu: Mo (from 3:1 to 1:3) were depicted in Fig. S5- S7. It revealed that, in addition to the diffraction peaks from the Ni foam substrate, the other set of peaks corresponds to Ni3S2, and the intensity of diffraction peaks progressively strengthens with increasing hydrothermal temperatures (Fig. S5). At a hydrothermal temperature of 120 °C, there are no evident morphological features on the surface (Fig. S6a-c), suggesting Ni3S2 without well-defined crystalline combined with XRD result. As revealed in Fig. S6d-f, tiny crystalline emerge, accompanied by an uneven and roughened surface as the hydrothermal temperature rises to 150 °C. Upon further increasing the hydrothermal temperature to 180 °C, the small clumps and clusters become clearly visible, and the formation of pores is imitated (Fig. S6g-i). Subsequently, at 200 °C, no substantial differences in the diffraction peaks are observed, indicating that the Ni3S2 are utterly crystallized (Fig. S6j-l). Similarly, with increasing the ratio of Mo, an obvious evolution of the surface framework was observed (Fig. S7). The transition progresses from an initial 3D petal-shaped structure characterized by a nanorod array structure, followed by the formation of a nanohybrid configuration, and ultimately culminates in the development of a densely packed structure resembling thick cones. The result illustrates that the desired micro-morphology can be effectively controlled by adjusting hydrothermal temperatures and the proportions of Cu and Mo metal in the synthesis process.

Moreover, changes in the morphology and structure of CQDs-Ni3S2@NF after doped Cu and Mo were shown in Fig. 2 . The CQDs-Ni3S2@NF displays a thin sheet structure with more interspace (Fig. 2a–c). As shown in Fig. S8a-c, it is evident that ultrafine nanoparticles of CQDs (∼4.5 nm) are clearly discernible within the CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, signed with red circles. These nanoparticles exhibit lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.254 nm, ascribing to the (120) plane of CQDs (Fig. S8d), providing obvious evidence of the successfully formation of CQDs nanoparticles on the surface of Ni3S2@NF. The elemental mapping image (Fig. S9) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum illustrate the uniform dispersion of C, S, and Ni elements throughout the CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. As shown in Fig. 2d–i, the CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF shows closely thick cones, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF takes on a spongy shape, whereas CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, without adding Cu and Mo precursors, displays a thin film structure with more interspace. Upon comparing these SEM images, it is noted that the addition of an appropriate amount of Cu or Mo precursors can efficiently modulate the morphology of the initial CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, resulting in the formation of a network of channels that facilitates the diffusion of electrolytes. That is, in contrast to the other control samples, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits a 3D network structure characterized by interwoven 2D nanosheets, which may anticipate to play a pivotal role in facilitating efficient charge transfer and ensuring structure stability.

XPS analysis was employed to characterize the state of the strong electronic interaction in the synthesized samples. The survey XPS spectra of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2 @NF, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF are depicted in Fig. 3 a. It is readily observable that C, Mo, and Cu peaks are located in CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF nanosheets, providing robust evidence of successfully doping of C, Mo, and Cu into the Ni3S2 lattices. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the Ni 2p XPS spectrum of CQDs-Mo-Cu/Ni3S2@NF possesses two peaks at binding energies of 856.32 eV and 873.00 eV, corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively. The peaks at 855.20 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 872.75 eV (Ni 2p1/2) are attributed to Ni2+, while the peaks at 856.43 eV and 874.46 eV belong to Ni3+ [36]. Additionally, a small peak at 853.18 eV corresponds to the Ni foam. And the two broad peaks at 861.09 and 879.22 eV are the typical satellite peaks. The Ni peak position in CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF is shifted to higher binding energy compared to CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2 @NF, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. Fig. 3c shows the peaks of S 2p at 162.25 and 163.49 eV, attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [37]. The broad peak at 168.46 eV corresponds to the satellite peak. Besides, in CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, a lower binding energy of S 2p is observed compared to CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. It is worth noting that the S 2p3/2 signal in CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF presents a negative shift of −0.08 eV relative to that in CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. In contrast, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF displays a positive shift of about 0.52 eV and CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF presents a positive shift of about 0.20 eV, revealing the electron-rich state of the S element. Evidently, this electron-rich S is beneficial to the adsorption of H* and further improves the HER activity [38]. In Fig. 3d, the peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 display positive shifts of 0.26 and 0.30 eV, respectively, compared to CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 3e, the Mo 3d XPS spectrum of CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF reveals that 231.89 and 232.09 eV corresponds to 3d5/2 of Mo4+, 228.63 and 232.1 eV corresponds to 3d3/2 of Mo6+, and the peaks at 226.31 belongs to S 2s [39]. The binding energies of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 in CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF have a positive shift by 0.31 eV and 0.23 eV, respectively, in comparison to CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF. As shown in Fig. 3f, three peaks are located at 284.46 eV, 285.54 eV, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to sp2-hybridized carbon (C–C), epoxy/hydroxyl carbon (C–O/C–O–C), and carbonyl carbon (OC–O), respectively [40]. Moreover, the binding energy has positive shifts upon Cu and Mo doping in CQDs-Ni3S2@NF. All of these observations fully declare the presence of strong electronic interactions between Cu, Mo, and CQDs-Ni3S2 in CQDs-Mo-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, leading to charge redistribution and further enhancing the water splitting process.

The systematic HER catalytic activities of all samples were investigated in 1 M KOH solution. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves indicate that 200 °C is regarded as the optimal hydrothermal temperature (Fig. S10) and the molar ratio between Cu and Mo of 3:2 shows the best HER activity (Fig. S11). Simultaneously, the optimum amount of l-cysteine is found to be 0.60g (Fig. S12). To provide a comparative reference, NF, CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, and Pt were also subjected to measurements. As unveiled in Fig. 4 a&b, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF electrodes display overpotentials of 131, 201, 253, and 325 mV at −10, −50, −100 and −200 mA·cm−2, respectively. These overpotentials are notably lower than those of 249, 417, 505 and 600 mV observed in NF. Moreover, they outperform CQDs-Ni3S2@NF (182, 337, 434, and 554 mV), CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF (176, 312, 393 and 502 mV), and CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF (172, 275, 342 and 444 mV). Especially, the HER activity of CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF surpasses that of Pt and samples without CQDs (Fig. S13) when current densities reach −10, −50, −100 and −200 mA·cm−2. This further verifies the exceptional performance of the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF electrode in the HER process. The values of double layer capacitance (C
dl ) vividly highlight the disparities in exposed active sites. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) is directly proportional to the Cdl, assessed by evaluating the CV curves at different scan rates (Fig. S14) through linear fitting of the points of ΔJ at 0.07 V vs. RHE within the proper potential range [41]. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF shows a smaller Tafel slope value of 72 mV·dec−1 compared to NF (214 mV·dec−1), CQDs-Ni3S2@NF ((201 mV·dec−1), CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF (194 mV·dec−1), CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2@NF (142 mV·dec−1) and Pt (196 mV·dec−1). Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4d, CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF demonstrates a relatively larger C
dl value (47.9 mF·cm−2) compared to NF (0.8 mF·cm−2), CQDs-Ni3S2@ NF ((1.0 mF·cm−2), CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF (4.2 mF·cm−2), and CQDs-Mo-Ni3S2 @NF (8.7 mF·cm−2). These results correspond to ECSA values of 1197.5, 20, 25, 105, and 217.5, respectively, further implying the presence of the richest exposed active sites and the highest intrinsic activity in CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF. The catalytic reaction kinetics of the electrode can be estimated using the corresponding Tafel slope. In alkaline media, the HER mechanism typically involves two steps: the Volmer reaction (H2O + e− + M = M-Hads + OH−) and either the Heyrovsky (H2O + e− + M-Hads = M + H2 + OH−) or the Tafel reaction (M-Hads + M-Hads = M + H2) [42]. These two different routes both encompass the adsorption of H2O molecules, the electrochemical reduction of H2O molecules into adsorbed hydrogen atoms (Hads) and hydroxyl ions (OH−), and the desorption of OH−, which is beneficial for forming Hads and subsequently evolving H2. As for CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, the Tafel slope of 72 mV·dec−1 indicates that it follows a Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway mechanism, with the rate-determining step being Hads desorption [43]. Surprisingly, when compared to conventional electrocatalysts from previous studies (Table S1), CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits brilliant HER properties.

The long-term stability stands as an important criterion for valuing the practical application quality of electro catalyst. The LSV curve remains nearly unchanged before and after a 30-h duration at the same current density, as illustrated in Fig. 4f. The chronoamperometry test demonstrates that the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF can maintain the current density of 10 mA·cm−2 over a period of 30 h almost without degradation. Impressively, even at a high current density of 50 mA·cm−2 a continuous hydrogen evolution over 30 h can also be achieved, which indicates the CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF a robust electrocatalyst. When comparing the peak positions of the Ni, S, Cu, and Mo elements before and after the HER stability test, it's evident that they remain consistent (Fig. S15). The high valence transition metal element can be quickly reduced back to its metallic states. More importantly, the doped CQDs induce the charge redistribution within the hybrids structure and act as a protective shield for the transition-metal-based material against oxidation/hydroxylation, ensuring its excellent durability [44].

The hydrophilic of the prepared catalysts, including CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, and CQD-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF, was assessed using contact angle measurement. In general, the initial adsorption of water molecules is a crucial step in the water splitting and a catalyst with higher hydrophilicity is more compatible with water. As revealed in Fig. S16, the contact angles of CQDs-Ni3S2@NF, CQDs-Cu-Ni3S2@NF, and CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF are 92°, 61°, and 0°, respectively. After being coated with Mo–Cu dual-doping, the liquid droplet instantaneously spreads into the porous structure, performing super hydrophilic properties. This super hydrophilic characteristic is particularly advantageous because, as water electrolysis takes place in an aqueous environment, a super hydrophilic electrode can make more effective contact with the electrolytes. This, in turn, fully exposes the active sites on the electrode surface, ultimately leading to the low activation energy of water dissociation.

To get an in-depth understanding of the intrinsic reasons for the improved HER catalytic activity, the electronic structures and adsorption energies of samples were calculated by DFT simulations. Models were constructed based on the (110) surface and the metal-doping sites are depicted in Fig. 5 a. The substitution energies for Cu, Mo, and dual metal-doping with these two metals were determined to be −3.38, −4.29, and −3.64 eV respectively, indicating the thermodynamic feasible of the metal-doping method. Building upon these configurations, the projecting density of states (PDOS) and crystal orbital Hamilton populations (COHP) were analyzed to examine the electronic structures, as shown in Fig. 5b. The continuous states crossing the Fermi level of DOS suggest that the doping method could maintain the metallic behavior. The pCOHP results demonstrated that the antibonding of Mo–S or Cu–S was shifted closer to the Fermi level, facilitating the electron transfer from H to metals [45,46]. According to the reaction pathways, the Gibbs free energy of H adsorption (ΔGH*) on the different sites of M − Ni3S2 was calculated to evaluate the HER performance (Table S2). Fig. 5c present the most stable ΔGH* curves, and the results suggest that the HER performance order is Cu–Mo–Ni3S2 > Mo–Ni3S2 > Cu–Ni3S2 > Ni3S2, aligning well our experimental results. The adsorption of H2O on the catalysts plays a vital role in alkaline condition, and its activation is considered the rate-determining step of the Volmer step in HER. Calculations of water adsorption energies on various sites of the doping Ni3S2 were performed to evaluate the energy barriers of H2O activation, following the Brønsted-Evans-Polanyi principle. As shown in Fig. 5d, water molecule preferred to adsorb on the Ni or doped-metal sites, and the H2O adsorption energies were generally more negative than that on bare Ni3S2, which further suggests that the metal-doping on Ni3S2 can effectively boost HER performance.

To further confirm the role of CQDs in improving HER performance, differential charge density and DOS were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5e&f. These analyses revealed that electrons tend to transfer from CQDs to CuMo–Ni3S2 via C–O–Ni (M) bond, formed by the metal sites of M−Ni3S2 and oxygen-containing functional groups (such as –OH group). The continuous and higher occupation levels in the DOS further verified that CQDs accelerate electron transfer from CQDs to Ni3S2, corroborating the experimental results.

In this work, we have successfully developed CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF utilizing a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach. The inclusion of Cu and Mo dopants is homogeneously distributed within the sulfide host, and this modification proves to be highly effective in tailoring the morphology, exposing additional active sites, optimizing the energetics of adsorption/desorption of intermediates, and enhancing the electrochemical performance. What's more, the incorporation of CQDs as a cocatalyst, combined with non-precious dual-metal dopants, further contributes significantly to the heightened catalytic activity due to their superior electrical conductivity and enhanced electron transfer capabilities. Consequently, the resulting CQDs-Cu-Mo-Ni3S2@NF exhibits optimized electrocatalytic activity for HER, which achieves a low overpotential of 131 mV at a current density of 10 mA·cm−2 and robust stability over a 30 h period in an alkaline environment, without any performance degradation. This novel one-step approach for designing CQDs loaded with Mo and Cu dual-doped catalysts may serve as a source of inspiration for constructing catalysts in various other catalytic applications.

Xiaomei Xu: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Bilu Yang: Data curation. Zhaodi Xu: Data curation. Qiaoling Mo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Xiaomei Shen: Writing – review & editing. Hu Cai: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21861026, 22075123, 22007041, 22367013). And this work was also supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20212BAB213019).
